title: Pyrography portrait shading
subtitle: Building tonal gradients for animal and figure portraits in pyrography
excerpt: Portrait pyrography differs from geometric or line work in one way: tone does everything and line does almost nothing. This technique covers the layer-by-layer approach to building tonal gradients; starting with the lightest areas, building darkest accents last; and the specific tip movements that produce fur, feather, and skin texture on wood.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for tonal grading in pokerwork portraiture; traditional European pyrography for animal and figure subject conventions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The art of decorating wood by burning the surface with a heated metal tip. In portrait work, tone is the primary descriptive element.
A flat curved pyrography tip for building graduated tones. Tilted on edge for a darker mark, laid flat for a lighter, broader one.
A round-tipped pyrography tip used for stippling: producing a uniform field of closely spaced dots to create fine texture.

content.text: Work in a well-ventilated room; burning produces light smoke. Portrait
pyrography

is not about drawing with a burner; it is about painting with heat. The distinction matters because portrait pyrography works in layers: each layer adds a small amount of tone; the cumulative effect of 4 to 6 passes produces the full tonal range from pale highlight to deep shadow. A nichrome wire burner is essential for this technique; temperature control between passes is what separates a portrait with convincing depth from one that looks flat.
The reference and the tonal map

Before burning, convert the reference image to greyscale and identify 5 tonal bands: white (no burn), light (10 to 20 percent burn coverage), mid (40 to 50 percent), dark (70 to 80 percent), and black (95 to 100 percent solid burn). Mark these zones on a tracing of the image in pencil. Transfer the tracing lightly to the panel. The pencil marks are guides for where each tonal band falls; the actual burning must follow the form, not the outline.
Layer-by-layer approach
First pass: light tone only. Set the temperature to produce the lightest intended mark on the back of the panel. Cover all areas except the white zones with a single pass of the
shading tip
. This establishes the lightest tone across the full portrait. Move the tip in the direction of the subject's texture (fur direction, feather direction) wherever possible.

Second and third passes: build the mid-tone. Increase temperature slightly (10 to 15 percent) and cover the mid and dark zones with additional passes. Do not go into the light zones. By the end of the third pass, the tonal separation between light and mid should be visible and convincing.

Fourth pass: dark tone. Increase temperature again and work only the dark zones. The shading tip at a higher temperature produces a mark that is visibly darker than the mid-tone from the same pressure and speed; this tonal jump creates depth.

Fifth pass: final dark accents. Use the writing tip at high temperature for the darkest marks; the pupil of the eye, the deepest shadow under the chin, the darkest fur in a recessed area. These marks should be burnt at maximum darkness and maximum precision. They are applied last because they cannot be lightened if misplaced.
Texture detail (optional): use the
ball stylus

at low temperature for fine stippled texture in fur or feather areas; a field of fine dots that reads as texture rather than a shaded mass. Work in the mid-tone zone; stippling in the dark zone is not visible enough to be useful.
Wood direction and tip movement

The shading tip moved along the grain produces a smoother tone than the same tip moved across the grain. For facial subjects, move the tip in the direction of hair or fur growth; outward from the face, following the growth lines. For feathers, move along the vane direction from quill to margin. Moving against the texture direction produces marks that read as crosshatch, which works for some textured backgrounds but not for smooth-skinned or silky-furred subjects.
Common mistakes
Darkening too early: applying dark tone before the mid-tone is established produces a flat portrait with insufficient tonal range between the mid and dark zones. Always build light before dark.

Inconsistent tip pressure: heavier pressure produces a darker mark from the same temperature. Keep pressure light and even throughout each pass; use temperature changes, not pressure changes, to build tone.

Leaving hard edge transitions between tonal zones: portrait subjects require soft gradients between zones. Blend the boundary between each zone by working back and forth across it with a lighter temperature after the zone is established.
